one_line_takeaway: Standard cross-validation inflates apparent generalization of activation-based prompt-attack classifiers by 8-16.5pp of AUC versus a stricter Leave-One-Dataset-Out protocol, because classifiers exploit dataset-identity shortcuts (up to 44% of top SAE features) that partially overlap with safety-relevant directions and resist seven standard domain-generalization fixes.
key_finding: Standard CV/held-out evaluation overestimates true (LODO) out-of-distribution generalization by 8.0-16.5 percentage points of pooled AUC across four model families (Llama-3.1-8B: 0.996 CV AUC vs 0.912 LODO AUC, 8.4pp gap; Gemma-3-27B: 0.999 vs 0.920, 8.0pp; Qwen-3.5-4B: 0.998 vs 0.845, 15.4pp; Qwen-3.5-2B: 0.998 vs 0.829, 16.5pp), with per-dataset accuracy gaps ranging 1.2% (safeguard) to 25.4% (jayavibhav) among the six datasets with official test splits. Weighted-by-mixed-class-only gap (excluding near-single-class BIPIA) is 12.5pp, ruling out single-class artifacts as the sole driver. A dataset-identity classifier trained on activations reaches 96.6% accuracy on SAE features (89.1% on raw layer-31, 86.4% on raw layer-27), showing datasets are trivially distinguishable in feature space. 28% (Llama-3.1-8B) to 44% (Gemma-3-27B) of the top-50 SAE features by coefficient magnitude are dataset shortcuts (retention threshold 0.5; sensitivity range 14%-56% across thresholds 0.3-0.7), and 42% of these shortcuts are 'context-dependent' -- they would not be caught by simple firing-ratio heuristics. Targeted removal of the top-16 dataset-predictive directions degrades LODO by 1.75pp aggregate versus -0.02+/-0.13pp for equal-rank random isotropic projections (p<1e-10), and the safety classifier's weight direction has 9.2% of its norm inside the dataset-predictive subspace versus the 0.39% expected under isotropy -- evidence of directional overlap (not proven causal) between dataset-identity and safety-relevant signal. None of seven tested domain-generalization mitigations (DANN, subspace projection, reweighting variants, class balancing, SAE shortcut zeroing) closes the gap; aggregate LODO deltas range from -1.75pp to +0.7pp. Under LODO, raw activations beat SAE features on Llama-3.1-8B (0.912 vs 0.838 pooled AUC) but SAE beats raw on Gemma-3-27B (76.2% vs 68.3% weighted accuracy), so the raw>SAE ordering is model-specific, not universal. At matched operating points the activation probe outperforms PromptGuard2/LlamaGuard/LLM-as-judge on Jailbreak (+30.6pp vs LlamaGuard), Indirect injection (+25.7pp), and Agentic attacks (covers this surface exclusively, since PG/LG lack tool-schema support), while LlamaGuard retains its lead on conversational Harmful-request refusals (97.4% vs 64.7%). LLM-as-judge is shown to be highly prompt-sensitive: across four prompt variants, indirect-injection TPR ranges from 11% (1.4% FPR) to 93% (18.9% FPR, non-viable due to 40.6%/91.1% FPR on benign OpenOrca/BIPIA). LODO-weighted SAE attributions (z_j*w_j*r_j) re-rank the top-20 explanatory features for 98.1% of 1,000 sampled inputs relative to unweighted attributions, with demoted features averaging 0.265 retention versus 0.990 for promoted (Cohen's d=5.60).
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution is not a new prompt-attack classifier but a new evaluation protocol: Leave-One-Dataset-Out (LODO) cross-validation for activation-based malicious-prompt classifiers, applied at the dataset level to a compiled benchmark of 18 datasets (105K samples, 47% malicious) spanning harmful requests, jailbreaks, direct/indirect prompt injection, and extraction attacks. Using linear probes (and an MLP) on residual-stream activations from four LLMs (Llama-3.1-8B, Gemma-3-27B, Qwen-3.5-2B/4B, plus a partial Llama-3.3-70B run), the authors show standard 5-fold CV / held-out-test-set evaluation systematically overestimates out-of-distribution generalization relative to LODO (where an entire dataset, not just a sample split, is withheld from training). They further introduce a per-feature LODO coefficient 'retention' metric on sparse-autoencoder (SAE) features to diagnose which learned features are dataset-specific shortcuts versus genuinely transferable, train a dataset-identity classifier to show datasets are trivially distinguishable in activation space, test whether dataset-identity and safety-relevant subspaces overlap via subspace projection, evaluate seven standard domain-generalization fixes (none close the gap), benchmark their activation probe against production guardrails (PromptGuard 2, LlamaGuard, Llama-as-Judge, ProtectAI Guard v2, Deepset v2) under LODO, and propose LODO-weighted SAE attributions (z_j * w_j * r_j) as more reliable per-prompt explanations. Code/framework released at github.com/maxf-zn/prompt-mining (not the trained checkpoints or activations).
threat_model: Defender-side, white-box setting: the defender has access to LLM hidden-state activations at inference time (to run an activation probe) but cannot modify the underlying model; this is contrasted with text-only baselines (PromptGuard 2, LlamaGuard, LLM-as-judge) that lack activation access. The attacker side is not novel in this paper -- no new attack technique is introduced. Attack categories covered are inherited from prior literature: harmful requests, jailbreaks (roleplay/framing prompt engineering to bypass safety), prompt injection including indirect injection embedded in tool outputs/emails/documents processed by agents, and extraction attacks targeting hidden system information. The paper explicitly disclaims robustness framing: LODO measures static distribution shift, not adversarial robustness against adaptive attackers (the Limitations section states this directly and cites Nasr et al. 2025's adaptive-attack bypasses of 12 defenses as a complementary but distinct axis).
limitations: The paper's own stated limitations: (1) White-box access requirement -- activation probes need model internals, precluding direct use with closed-source APIs (mitigations suggested: open-weight sidecar classifiers, or distillation into text-based classifiers, both left as future work). (2) LODO measures static distribution shift, not adversarial robustness -- a classifier with a small CV-LODO gap can still be defeated by an adaptive attacker; this is explicitly not addressed. (3) Dataset-choice dependence -- LODO treats each constituent dataset as a domain, so it can understate the gap when datasets share methodology or over/understate it when datasets are unrepresentative of production traffic; practitioners must choose datasets spanning distinct attack surfaces. (4) Computational cost -- LODO requires training K classifiers, tractable for logistic regression (minutes/fold) but potentially prohibitive for expensive fine-tuning. (5) Interpretability scope -- SAE feature attributions reveal what the classifier detects, not whether the underlying LLM would comply; feature contributions are not causal interventions, and human evaluation of LODO-weighted explanation rankings is left to future work. (6) No mitigation provided -- the paper establishes and diagnoses the gap and shows it resists seven standard DG interventions but does not offer a method that closes it; they position this as an intentionally diagnostic (not solution) contribution. The paper also notes ablating the 14 identified SAE shortcut features only moves pooled AUC by -0.1pp because redundant features compensate, so they explicitly do NOT claim identified shortcuts causally explain the full gap -- the subspace-overlap finding is offered as the more direct (though still correlational, not proven causal) evidence.

quantitative_results: Llama-3.1-8B: 5-fold CV AUC 0.996, held-out test AUC 0.997, LODO pooled AUC 0.912 (8.4pp CV-LODO gap)
Gemma-3-27B: CV AUC 0.999, LODO AUC 0.920 (8.0pp gap)
Qwen-3.5-4B: CV AUC 0.998, LODO AUC 0.845 (15.4pp gap)
Qwen-3.5-2B: CV AUC 0.998, LODO AUC 0.829 (16.5pp gap)
Partial Llama-3.3-70B: LODO AUC 0.910 (no paired CV reported)

Per-dataset test-vs-LODO accuracy gaps: mosscap +20.1% (99.5% to 79.4%), jayavibhav +25.4% (94.5% to 69.1%), qualifire +18.0% (95.8% to 77.8%), enron +16.6% (99.2% to 82.6%), safeguard +1.2% (97.9% to 96.7%), deepset +2.5% (80.2% to 77.7%)
Dataset-identity classifier accuracy: 96.6% on SAE (layer 27) features, 89.1% on raw layer-31 activations, 86.4% on raw layer-27 activations
28% of top-50 SAE features are dataset shortcuts on Llama-3.1-8B; 44% on Gemma-3-27B (retention threshold 0.5; range 14%-56% across thresholds 0.3-0.7); 42% of shortcuts are context-dependent

Targeted subspace projection (top-16 dataset-predictive directions removed): LODO degrades by 1.75pp vs -0.02+/-0.13pp for random equal-rank isotropic projections (p<1e-10); safety-classifier weight has 9.2% of its norm in the dataset-predictive subspace vs 0.39% theoretical isotropic baseline
Seven domain-generalization fixes tested; aggregate LODO deltas range -1.75pp (subspace projection) to +0.7pp (SAE shortcut zeroing) -- none close the 8.4pp Llama-8B gap
Gemma-3-27B: SAE weighted accuracy 76.2% vs raw 68.3% under LODO (reversal of Llama ordering)

Detection rate by category at threshold 0.9 (probe) vs baselines: Jailbreak probe 59.5% vs LlamaGuard 28.9% (+30.6pp); Indirect probe 53.1% vs LlamaGuard 27.4% (+25.7pp); Agentic probe 91.5% (PG/LG inapplicable, no tool-schema support); Mixed probe 74.9% vs LlamaGuard 38.8% (+36.1pp); Harmful: LlamaGuard 97.4% vs probe 64.7%; Benign FPR: probe 3.7% vs LlamaGuard 3.0%

LLM-as-judge prompt-variant sensitivity: indirect-injection TPR ranges 11% (1.4% FPR) to 93% (18.9% FPR, with 40.6% FPR on OpenOrca and 91.1% FPR on benign BIPIA -- non-viable); at 3.5% FPR operating point probe outperforms LJ by +22pp on indirect injection (68.2% vs 46.0%)
ProtectAI Guard v2: 4.2% FPR but 0% TPR on harmful requests and 8% TPR on indirect injection; Deepset v2: 99.8% TPR but 82.4% FPR
LODO-weighted vs standard SAE attribution re-ranks top-20 features for 98.1% of 1,000 sampled inputs; demoted features mean retention 0.265 vs promoted 0.990 (Cohen's d=5.60, p<0.001)
Training-free Mahalanobis-distance baseline (LPM) trails logistic regression by 5.8pp weighted accuracy under LODO
baselines_compared: TaskTracker (Abdelnabi et al., 2025a) -- discussed as closest activation-probe baseline in Related Work, not independently re-run under LODO in the main results
